EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope snow showers developed during the early morning hours of March 17th and continued through the late evening along the western slopes of the Allegheny Front in the wake of a strong cold front. The cold front and associated low pressure system brought several inches of snow to the region on March 16th before upslope showers developed several hours later. Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 5 inches across Allegany and Washington Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotters reported between 3 and 5 inches of snow near Hancock, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving across the Ohio Valley produced freezing rain across the region with one quarter inch of ice accumulating in protected valley locations of Garrett county Maryland.

Snow showers developed during the evening hours of March 3rd and continued through the morning hours of March 4th along and west of the Allegheny Front in the wake of a strong cold front. |A SKYWARN Spotter reported 2 inches of snowfall in Frostburg, MD.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SKYWARN Spotter reported 2 inches of snowfall in Frostburg, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season snow developed over lower southern Maryland as a low pressure system deepened off the Delmarva Coast late April 6th. Snow showers began early on April 7th, with the heaviest snow falling between 2 and 6 AM. Snow amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches, with highest amounts reported across St. Mary's County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRahs Observers reported between 1 and 2 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRahs Observers and Trained Spotters reported between 1 and 3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of Extratropical Storm Noel tracking up off the Mid Atlantic Coast and High Pressure building into the region from the northwest produced strong northeast winds over portions of the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust of 51 mph was measured at 44009. Wind gust of 40 mph was measured at OXB.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds behind a cold front associated with strong low pressure caused damage in Garrett county Maryland, and Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down south of Mc Henry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds behind a cold front associated with strong low pressure caused damage in Garrett county Maryland, and Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down in the Oakland area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving area of low pressure produced heavy snow across Garrett county Maryland and parts of Northern West Virginia. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 9 inches across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 8 inches of snow in Friendsville with over 6 inches in other parts of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of strong low pressure pushed a cold front across the region with heavy rain. Combined with some snow melt this produced flooding across portions of Northern West Virginia, Garrett county Maryland, and Greene and Fayette counties in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County official reported multiple streams and creeks out of their banks with numerous roads closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure brought freezing rain to parts of Northwest Pennsylvania and Garrett county Maryland as low level cold air remained entrenched across these regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported one quarter inch of ice accumulation in Friendsville and in valley locations across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the lower Mississippi River Valley on December 15th and moved north across the Mid Atlantic on December 16th. Precipitation began during the late afternoon of the 15th as a mixture of snow and sleet. Warm air aloft then pushed over the region during the late evening and overnight hours on the 16th, changing the precipitation to a mixture of sleet and freezing rain. Significant accumulations of ice, snow and sleet were reported across northern Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotters and Cooperative Observers reported a quarter inch of ice and up to an inch of snow in western Allegany County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper system moved quickly across the Mid Atlantic on December 5th.  This was the first snow of the season for much of the region. This storm caused significant traffic impacts during the morning commute across the Washington DC and Baltimore metro areas. The highest snowfall accumulations were measured across northern Maryland from Allegany County east to Carroll County. Snow amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches across lower southern Maryland north into the Washington and southern Baltimore suburbs, and up to 7 inches in far western Allegany County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observers and trained spotters reported between 4 and 5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the Mid Atlantic early on December 3rd. This brought gusty winds to portions of central Maryland through much of the day. Many automated observations measured wind gusts between 40 and 55 mph. Damages consisted of trees and power lines brought down by the winds. Some trees and power lines fell onto homes and cars. ||Upslope snow showers developed during the late morning hours along the western face of the Allegheny Front in western Maryland. Snow amounts ranged from 3 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 1 mile east of Frostburg measured 3.5 of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over eastern North Carolina spread precipitation across the Mid Atlantic on December 30th. As the low pulled north and intensified off Cape Cod, colder air was brought across the region, causing rain to turn to a wintry mix and finally snow across the region during the evening and overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Trained Spotter measured 1.8 inches of snow in Hancock, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed across portions of north-central Maryland during the early morning hours of December 31st. Visibilities were often reduced to a quarter mile or less. Temperatures during the overnight hours fell into the lower 30s, causing fog to freeze onto some surfaces. The fog and glaze of ice caused significant travel problems. Over 100 accidents were reported on Interstate 70, US 340 and US 15. One fatality was reported in Frederick County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog and freezing fog was reported by the ASOS at the Hagerstown Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed across portions of north-central Maryland during the early morning hours of December 31st. Visibilities were often reduced to a quarter mile or less. Over 100 accidents were reported on Interstate 70, US 340 and US 15. One fatality was reported in Frederick County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog and freezing fog was reported by the AWOS at the Frederick Municipal Airport. Dense freezing fog and icy roadways caused numerous travel problems in Frederick County. Over 100 vehicle crashes were reported during the early and mid morning hours. The westbound lanes of Interstate 70 were closed for at least 9 hours due to a crash. A newspaper reported that a local firefighter was killed about 4:30 AM on the US 15 bridge over the Potomac River at Point of Rocks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system moved from the Ohio Valley to off the North Carolina coast. The system produced one half inch to one inch of snow during the evening of February 6th and early morning of February 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One half inch of snow fell in Cambridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak low pressure system moved from the Ohio Valley to off the North Carolina coast. The system produced one half inch to one inch of snow during the evening of February 6th and early morning of February 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One half inch to one inch of snow fell in Salisbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow in the wake of a weak clipper system allowed very cold air to stream across the still unfrozen waters of Lake Michigan and transport moisture into the higher elevations of the Allegheny Front of western Maryland. Cooperative Observers and trained spotters reported total new snow accumulations of between 3 and 4 inches from Frostburg to Eckhart Mines in Allegany County, Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observers reported between 3 and 4 inches of snow in Frostburg and Eckhart Mines in western Allegany County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front pushed through the Mid Atlantic from the mid west during the evening hours on February 3rd. Northwest winds behind the front gusted to 40 mph. The combination of cold temperatures and strong winds produced wind chill values from 5 to 15 below zero during the overnight hours from February 3rd through February 6th.  The morning of Monday, February 5th was the coldest of the stretch. Temperatures dipped below zero in Frostburg, MD., according to Cooperative Observer reports.|Annie Mae Anderson, 81, of Silver Spring, MD., was found dead during the morning of February 5th in a wooded area behind her home. Anderson, who suffered from dementia, apparently wandered away from her home without her coast. Cause of death was exposure to the cold weather. |Allegany County schools were closed Monday, February 5th due to the cold.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Annie Mae Anderson, 81, of Silver Spring, MD., was found dead during the morning of February 5th in a wooded area behind her home. Anderson, who suffered from dementia, apparently wandered away from her home without her coast. Cause of death was exposure to the cold weather.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated Surface Observations reported dense fog with visibilities reduced to one quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure over the Mid Atlantic brought clear skies and light winds to much of northern and central Maryland. This allowed for overnight low temperatures to fall to around 30 degrees. This freeze combined with a continuing drought brought an end to the growing season.
A large and powerful storm system over New England brought high elevation snow to the ridges of Southwest Pennsylvania, Northern West Virgina, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and the NWS COOP observer reported 6 inches or more of snow across the highest elevations of Garrett county.

Local airports and automated observations reported fog reducing visibility to a quarter mile or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated surface observations in Frederick County reported dense fog reducing visibility to one quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought conditions persisted through October. In early October, rainfall deficits totaled nearly 10 inches. However, a series of low pressure systems late in the month brought between 3 and 6 inches of rainfall to slightly reduce those deficits. Many counties and cities posted both voluntary and mandatory water restrictions throughout the month. Just before the needed rainfall towards the end of the month, the National Drought Monitor listed portions of Montgomery and Charles Counties under Extreme Drought conditions. Damage estimates were not available.

Intense low pressure off the Mid Atlantic Coast produced very strong winds across portions of the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust of 48 mph was measured at OXB.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with an upper level low pressure system to trigger numerous showers and thunderstorms central Maryland during the afternoon and evening of July 29th.  Several of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds in Prince Georges and Frederick Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported trees down in the city of Frederick as well as the southeast section of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moving east across the Ohio Valley stalled as it moved over the Mid Atlantic. This allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across the region during the afternoon of July 16th. Many of these storms became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds that downed large trees and power lines. One severe thunderstorm that moved across Harford County produced a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calvert County Emergency Management relayed a public report of quarter size hail southwest of Saint Leonard, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A portion of the Mid Atlantic hydrologic service area experienced severe agricultural drought conditions from mid July through August 14th. This area extended from Washington County south east across the southern Baltimore metro into Calvert County. Using a combination of rainfall measurements and radar precipitation estimates, some locations averaged rainfall totals as high as 6 inches below normal. This led to some jurisdictions restricting water use. Damage estimates were not available. Several locations were included in primary natural disaster areas due to reductions in farm production. Sources of information included the Maryland Farm Service Agency and the National Drought Monitor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist air mass settled over the Mid Atlantic as a strong cold front approached the region from the Ohio Valley on July 19th. This allowed scattered thunderstorms to develop across north central Virginia during the early afternoon hours and spread east into Maryland through the evening. Storms became better organized as they moved east across lower southern Maryland, eventually forming a bow echo. Surface winds with the bow echo were measured between 60 and 80 mph. These strong thunderstorm winds downed numerous trees and power lines across lower southern Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur Radio Operators reported trees down in LaPlata. Law Enforcement Officers reported trees down on Cobb Island and the Route 301 Bridge was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with an upper level low pressure system to trigger thunderstorms in Harford County during the early morning hours of July 29th. The first thunderstorm became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail. A second storm followed close behind and actually became a wet microburst. Significant wind damage was reported in Bel Air, MD.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Harford County Emergency Management reported that a roof made of aluminum corrugated sheet metal was torn off building in downtown Bel Air in the 100 block of Business Route 1 and Archer Street. A large party tent was blown down nearby when its support ropes were snapped.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey concluded that an EF1 tornado on the Enhanced Fujita Scale occurred near Fallston, MD., on July 16th. Maximum path width was 150 yards and the length was approximately 4 miles. There was extensive tree damage, with some softwood trees snapped and many hardwoods toppled. Structural damage observed was limited to trees and limbs falling onto structures. Many power lines were also downed. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system drifting northwest towards the Great Lakes and increase southerly flow at the surface combine to trigger isolated showers and thunderstorms across western Maryland. One storm moving over Allegany County became severe, downing trees and power lines in Cumberland, MD.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Allegany County Emergency Management reported trees and power lines down in Cumberland. A local newspaper reported multiple trees and power lines down as well as large hail from Barton to Cumberland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense nor'easter brought strong winds to the Eastern Shore on the 16th. The strongest winds occurred as the nor'easter pulled northeast of the region on the 16th from the early morning into the afternoon. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph. The gusty northwest winds on Monday the 16th caused scattered power outages for both Delmarva Power and Choptank Electric Cooperative.|Peak wind gusts included 54 mph in Salisbury (Dorchester County), 53 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport, 48 mph in Easton (Talbot County), 47 mph in Annapolis (Anne Arundel County) and 46 mph in Chesapeake City (Cecil County) and Tolchester Beach (Kent County). |The nor'easter low pressure system initially formed in the southern Rockies on the 12th and moved to Arkansas on the morning of the 14th. At 8 p.m. EDT on the 14th, there were two low pressure systems, one in Kentucky and the other in western Alabama. The southern low pressure system became the predominate one overnight and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 15th, it (996 millibar low) was located in western North Carolina. As the 15th continued, it moved northeast and intensified rapidly. At 2 p.m. EDT the nor'easter (989 millibars) was near Raleigh, North Carolina. At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.

As the 15th continued, it moved northeast and intensified rapidly. At 2 p.m. EDT the nor'easter (989 millibars) was near Raleigh, North Carolina. At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.

An intense nor'easter brought strong winds to the Eastern Shore on the 16th. The strongest winds occurred as the nor'easter pulled northeast of the region on the 16th from the early morning into the afternoon. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. From early morning of the 13th to early morning on the 14th a large and powerful storm system moved east from the western Ohio Valley to the Mid Atlantic states. Across western Pennsylvania, much of southeast Ohio, parts of northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland, the storm system produced heavy snow of 6 to 12 inches, sleet accumulations of a half inch to 3 inches, and freezing rain accumulations from one quarter to one half inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell across Garrett county by mid afternoon with a storm total of 13 inches. 2 to 3 inches of sleet also fell with the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Nor'Easter impacted the Mid Atlantic region on April 15th and 16th. Low pressure off the southeastern coast moved north along the Delmarva. Heavy rain spread north across the region early April 15th, causing flooding by the afternoon and evening. As the low pressure area pushed north and intensified, colder air advanced from the northwest. This allowed rain to change to snow along the upslope region of the Allegheny Front. Winds also increased in the wake of the low, downing trees and power lines across central and lower southern Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newspapers reported several roads closed due to flooding across the eastern portion of the county. Heavy rains caused a mudslide which closed the Baltimore/Washington Parkway near Linthicum. A Trained Spotter reported flooding at Fort Meade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November 2007 brought the return of below normal precipitation to the Maryland Eastern Shore. On a county weighted average, November rainfall ranged from 0.7 inches in Caroline County to 2.2 inches in Cecil County. Normal is around 3.4 inches. ||The drought watch remained in effect for all of the Maryland Eastern Shore. A drought watch calls for a voluntary reduction in water consumption of five percent.||Actual monthly precipitation totals included 0.89 inches in Salisbury (Wicomico County), which was 2.41 inches drier than normal and 1.52 inches at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport which was 1.60 inches drier than normal.

A warm and unstable air mass across western Maryland created an environment conducive for isolated thunderstorms to develop. A few of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds in Allegany County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported a few trees down in Cumberland, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed across the Mid Atlantic on the evening of June 8th. Ahead of the front, a warm and unstable airmass allowed scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop over northern and central Maryland during the afternoon and spread southeast into lower southern Maryland during the evening. Some of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds that downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported power lines down in Wheaton, MD.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Officials from the Montgomery County Emergency Operations Center reported trees and power lines down in Kensington, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm area of high pressure built across the Mid Atlantic in late September. A cold front moved across the region on September 27, triggering numerous showers and thunderstorms across western and central Maryland. Some of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Trained Spotter reported nickel size hail in Cresaptown, MD.

A storm in Allegany County became severe, producing damaging winds in Cumberland, MD.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in Cumberland, MD.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported trees down on a vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Three separate mesoscale convective systems moved across Southeast Ohio, Western Pennsylvania, Northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Widespread wind damage, flash flooding, and an EF0 tornado occurred during the event. The tornado touched down in the West End of Pittsburgh. Significant flash flooding occurred in Millvale in Allegheny county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported trees down south of Friendsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds buffeted the Maryland Eastern Shore during the second half of the morning and the afternoon on the 3rd. Highest wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and downed trees and wires caused power outages.||Delmarva Power and Light reported about 4,400 homes and business lost power in Cecil and Harford Counties.  All power was restored by 6 p.m. EST. Wind restrictions were in place on the William Preston Lane Junior Bridge in Queen Anne's County.  Empty house trailers and box trailers were prohibited from crossing the bridge. The strong winds blew over road signs and one street light in Easton (Talbot County).  One downed tree also caused isolated power outages.||Specific wind gusts included 54 mph in Salisbury (Wicomico County), 53 mph in Ridgely (Caroline County), 47 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport and 46 mph in Tolchester Beach (Kent County). The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between a low pressure system that was intensifying as it was moving through the Saint Lawrence Valley and a high pressure system building toward the northeast from the Southern Plains.

An Alberta Clipper low pressure system dropped two to four inches of snow across the Maryland Eastern Shore on the 5th. Snow began during the morning and ended during the late evening on the 5th. Some rain mixed in with the snow along southern sections of the Eastern Shore during the afternoon of the 5th. While the snow was never heavy, most of the accumulating snow occurred during two spurts, one in the early afternoon and the second during the early evening.  ||Specific accumulations included 3.8 inches in Colora (Cecil County), 3.0 inches in Ridgely (Caroline County) and Stevensville (Queen Anne's County), 2.5 inches in Easton (Talbot County) and 2.0 inches in Conowingo (Cecil County).||As the name implies, the low pressure that caused the snow moved quickly from southern Alberta at 7 a.m. EST on the 4th to the central Ohio Valley at 7 a.m. EST on the 5th. By sunrise on the 6th, it was already well east of the Virginia coast.

A low pressure system developed over the lower Mississippi River Valley on December 15th and moved north across the Mid Atlantic on December 16th. Precipitation began during the late afternoon of the 15th as a mixture of snow and sleet. Warm air aloft then pushed over the region during the late evening and overnight hours on the 16th, changing the precipitation to a mixture of sleet and freezing rain. Significant accumulations of ice, snow and sleet were reported across northern Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotters and Cooperative Observers reported a half inch of ice and up to an inch of snow in Washington County.

A low pressure system moving across the Mid Atlantic brought a mixture of rain and freezing rain to portions of northern and western Maryland. Sub-freezing temperatures at the surface combined with a layer of warmer air aloft caused rain to change over to freezing rain. Ice accumulations between one quarter and one half inch were reported in western Allegany County, with lesser amounts further east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 0.55 of ice accretion 1 mile east of Frostburg, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that moved across the Mid Atlantic on December 15th deepened off the New England coast on December 16th.  As the storm intensified, gusty westerly winds developed. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were measured across the region. Damages were intensified in areas that received significant ice accumulations. Numerous downed trees and power lines caused extended power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Baltimore/Washington International Airport measured a wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of high pressure was anchored over southeastern Canada on December 15th. This high gradually moved eastward during the day, however it wedged itself down into the Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a storm system became more organized across the Gulf Coast states. This storm tracked northeastward from the 15th to the 16th. As the main storm tracked into Ohio on the 16th, a secondary area of low pressure developed near southern Delaware during that morning. Strong winds resulted as the storm intensified, mostly as the storm itself began to depart during the evening of the 16th. Peak wind gusts averaged between 45 and 50 mph and included 49 mph in Tolchester Beach (Kent County), 48 mph in Salisbury (Wicomico County), 46 mph in Ridgely (Caroline County) and 41 mph in Chesapeake City (Cecil County).

The unseasonably dry November led to the drought watch being upgraded to a drought warning for Caroline, Kent, Queen Annes and Talbot Counties. A drought warning calls for a voluntary reduction in water consumption of ten to fifteen percent. The drought watch remained in effect for Cecil County. A drought watch calls for a voluntary reduction in water consumption of five percent. Conditions though improved in December as it was wetter than normal throughout the Eastern Shore. By the end of the month the drought warning was downgraded to a drought watch for all of the Eastern Shore counties.||On a county weighted average, December precipitation ranged from 3.7 inches in Talbot County to 5.4 inches in Cecil County. Normal is around 3.6 inches. The December monthly precipitation total at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport was 4.03 inches, 0.68 inches wetter than normal and in Salisbury (Wicomico County) was 4.03 inches, 0.31 inches wetter than normal.

A strong cold front moved across the Mid Atlantic early on December 3rd. This brought gusty winds to portions of central Maryland through much of the day. Many automated observations measured wind gusts between 40 and 55 mph. Damages consisted of trees and power lines brought down by the winds. Some trees and power lines fell onto homes and cars. ||Upslope snow showers developed during the late morning hours along the western face of the Allegheny Front in western Maryland. Snow amounts ranged from 3 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Newspaper reported over 2,000 residents and 2 schools without power when high winds brought trees down onto power lines. No injuries or accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving out of the central plains weakened as it pushed east across the forecast area. After snow began to fall across the region, warm air at low levels moved in above sub-freezing temperatures at the surface. Light to moderate snow showers caused minor accumulations during the late morning hours of January 21 before snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain and then changed over to freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of January 22. SKYWARN Spotters and Cooperative Observers reported a mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain and drizzle, with total accumulations ranging from 1 to 4 inches across the region. |Multiple car accidents between 3:00 PM and 4:00 PM on January 21 closed portions of Interstate 95 and the Baltimore-Washington Parkway. Minor injuries were reported. Minor accidents were also reported on the elevated portions of the Jones Falls Expressway. Montgomery County authorities reported nearly 150 calls between 2:00 PM and 6:00 PM for traffic incidents. |In Frederick County, MD., dozens of traffic accidents were called into state police; ten of those resulted in minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Frostburg, MD, reported 3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed across the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon of January 30. Upslope snow showers persisted over the Allegheny Front and Potomac Highlands through the morning of January 29. Light snow showers producing accumulations between 1 and 2 inches occurred across western and central Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative Observer in Frederick, MD, 1.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper system moved quickly across the Mid Atlantic on December 5th.  This was the first snow of the season for much of the region. This storm caused significant traffic impacts during the morning commute across the Washington DC and Baltimore metro areas. The highest snowfall accumulations were measured across northern Maryland from Allegany County east to Carroll County. Snow amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches across lower southern Maryland north into the Washington and southern Baltimore suburbs, and up to 7 inches in far western Allegany County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters measured between 3 and 5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the lower Mississippi River Valley on December 15th and moved north across the Mid Atlantic on December 16th. Significant accumulations of ice, snow and sleet were reported across northern Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative Observer measured an inch of ice in Wolfsville, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that passed to the south of Maryland helped produce a wintry mix of precipitation from the late afternoon of the 21st into the early morning of the 22nd along the Maryland Eastern Shore. Snowfall accumulations were generally two inches or less and ice accretions were less than one-tenth of an inch. Untreated roads were very hazardous as air temperatures were below freezing throughout the event. |Snow began falling during the late afternoon of the 21st. As warmer air moved in aloft, the snow changed to freezing rain and sleet during the middle of the evening of the 21st on the southern Eastern Shore, but took until near the end of the precipitation event for the change to occur in northern Cecil County.|Actual snow accumulations included 2.2 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County) and 0.3 inches at the Conowingo Dam (Cecil County). The precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that developed along the western Gulf Coast on the 20th. Strong northwest to west winds occurred during the daytime hours on January 20th across the Maryland Eastern Shore. It was caused by an intense pressure gradient (large difference in surface pressure over a relatively small area) between a powerful low pressure system (960 millibars at 7 a.m. EST on the 20th) moving through the Saint Lawrence Valley and a high pressure system building east from the Midwest. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and included 55 mph in Easton (Talbot County) and 51 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport.

A low pressure system developed over the high plains of eastern Colorado on February 23rd and pushed east to the Ohio Valley by February 25th. Schools were delayed Monday, February 26th, in Montgomery and Prince Georges Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotters in central and eastern Allegany County reported between 4 and 5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved out of the Southern Plains and strengthened off the southeast coast February 12th through 14th, bringing accumulating wintry precipitation to much of Maryland beginning during the afternoon and evening hours of February 12th and continued through the early morning hours of February 14th. The heaviest precipitation occurred February 13th as the low pressure system intensified off the coast. |Snow and sleet accumulations ranged from 1 to 9 inches and ice accumulations ranged from a tenth to three quarters of an inch. This mix of sleet, snow and freezing rain created a very hard and thick layer of ice. Many snow plows were not equipped to handle such heavy precipitation, leading to longer wait times for roadways and sidewalks to be cleared. Across lower southern Maryland, freezing rain was more of a problem. Ice coated trees and power lines, causing over 100,000 power outages. Several roadways were closed due to downed trees. Those open roadways were snarled with traffic and dozens of minor car accidents. Schools were closed for much of the week. Restaurants and florists reported reduced Valentines��� Day sales due to the hazardous road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotters reported 3 to 4 inches of snow and sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass that originated near the North Pole invaded the Maryland Eastern Shore on the 5th and 6th. The combination of the unseasonably cold air and gusty northwest winds produced wind chill factors as low as zero to 10 degrees below zero during the mornings of the 5th and 6th. The lowest temperatures occurred during the morning of the 6th and were around 10 degrees. The unseasonably cold weather caused many pipes and water meters to freeze across the Eastern Shore. The municipal water system in Millington (Kent County) developed leaks. A water main burst in Chesapeake City (Cecil County). Actual lowest temperatures included 8 degrees at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport, 10 degrees in Easton (Talbot County), 11 degrees in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County) and Salisbury (Wicomico County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm that featured mixed precipitation affected the Maryland Eastern Shore on the 25th. Snowfall accumulations averaged 2 to 5 inches with up to around one quarter of an inch of ice accruing on exposed surfaces in Cecil County. Precipitation began around Noon EST. It started as snow in the northern part of the Eastern Shore. In the southern part of the Eastern Shore precipitation started as rain, but changed quickly to snow. The snow fell heavy at times later that afternoon, particularly in Caroline, Queen Anne's and Kent Counties. Enough warm air came in aloft for precipitation to change to sleet and freezing rain late in the afternoon and early in the evening from south to north. Precipitation then changed to plain rain during by the early evening in Talbot and Caroline Counties, but close to Midnight EST on the 26th in Cecil County. The rain ended well before sunrise on the 26th. |Untreated roads and walkways were hazardous. The snow did accumulate on road surfaces as the weather had remained unseasonably cold. Cecil County reported the most accidents as the State Police responded to 26 mainly minor ones. |Snowfall accumulations included 4.5 inches in Henderson (Caroline County), 4.0 inches in Rock Hall (Kent County), 3.9 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County), 3.0 inches in St Michaels (Talbot County) and at the Conowingo Dam (Cecil County) and 2.0 inches in Elkton (Cecil County) and Cordova (Talbot County).|The low pressure system that caused the winter storm moved northeast from the foothills of the central Rockies on the morning of the 24th. At 7 p.m. EST on the 24th it reached Kansas City, Kansas City and then northeastern Iowa at 7 a.m. EST on the 25th. A secondary low pressure system started forming on the primary low's warm front in South Carolina during the early afternoon of the 25th. At 7 p.m. EST the secondary low was near Raleigh, North Carolina, while the primary low pressure system was weakening near Chicago, Illinois. At 1 a.m. EST on the 26th, the secondary low pressure system was just east of the Delaware coast and at 7 a.m. EST that morning was already well southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts. While warm air continued to move in aloft because of the circulation associated with the primary low pressure system, the formation and deepening of the secondary low pressure system slowed the advancement of warmer air near the surface and prolonged the duration of sleet and freezing rain well inland.

Restaurants and florists reported reduced Valentines��� Day sales due to the hazardous road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm that featured mixed precipitation affected the Maryland Eastern Shore on the 25th. Snowfall accumulations averaged 2 to 5 inches with up to around one quarter of an inch of ice accruing on exposed surfaces in Cecil County. Precipitation began around Noon EST. It started as snow in the northern part of the Eastern Shore. In the southern part of the Eastern Shore precipitation started as rain, but changed quickly to snow. The snow fell heavy at times later that afternoon, particularly in Caroline, Queen Anne's and Kent Counties. Enough warm air came in aloft for precipitation to change to sleet and freezing rain late in the afternoon and early in the evening from south to north. Precipitation then changed to plain rain during by the early evening in Talbot and Caroline Counties, but close to Midnight EST on the 26th in Cecil County. The rain ended well before sunrise on the 26th. |Untreated roads and walkways were hazardous. The snow did accumulate on road surfaces as the weather had remained unseasonably cold. Cecil County reported the most accidents as the State Police responded to 26 mainly minor ones. |Snowfall accumulations included 4.5 inches in Henderson (Caroline County), 4.0 inches in Rock Hall (Kent County), 3.9 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County), 3.0 inches in St Michaels (Talbot County) and at the Conowingo Dam (Cecil County) and 2.0 inches in Elkton (Cecil County) and Cordova (Talbot County).|The low pressure system that caused the winter storm moved northeast from the foothills of the central Rockies on the morning of the 24th. At 7 p.m. EST on the 24th it reached Kansas City, Kansas City and then northeastern Iowa at 7 a.m. EST on the 25th. A secondary low pressure system started forming on the primary low's warm front in South Carolina during the early afternoon of the 25th. At 7 p.m. EST the secondary low was near Raleigh, North Carolina, while the primary low pressure system was weakening near Chicago, Illinois. At 1 a.m. EST on the 26th, the secondary low pressure system was just east of the Delaware coast and at 7 a.m. EST that morning was already well southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts. While warm air continued to move in aloft because of the circulation associated with the primary low pressure system, the formation and deepening of the secondary low pressure system slowed the advancement of warmer air near the surface and prolonged the duration of sleet and freezing rain well inland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving Alberta Clipper low pressure system dropped 1.5 to 3.5 inches of snow across much of the Maryland Eastern Shore overnight on the 6th into the morning of the 7th. Slightly lower accumulations occurred in Cecil County. Snow began falling around 11 p.m. EST on the 6th and ended between 6 a.m. and 8 a.m. EST that morning. Temperatures were below freezing as the snow fell and it quickly accumulated on all surfaces. Untreated roadways and walkways were all slippery. Specific accumulations included 3.5 inches in Chestertown (Kent County), 2.0 inches in Galena (Kent County), Henderson (Caroline County) and St. Michaels (Talbot County), 1.5 inches in Denton (Caroline County) and Easton (Talbot County) and 1.0 inch in Elkton (Cecil County). The Alberta Clipper responsible for the snow raced from South Dakota around sunrise on the 6th to just east of the southern Delmarva Peninsula on the morning of the 7th. The low pressure system was moving too fast to drop heavier snow across the region.

An arctic air mass that originated near the North Pole invaded the Maryland Eastern Shore on the 5th and 6th. The combination of the unseasonably cold air and gusty northwest winds produced wind chill factors as low as zero to 10 degrees below zero during the mornings of the 5th and 6th. The lowest temperatures occurred during the morning of the 6th and were around 10 degrees. The unseasonably cold weather caused many pipes and water meters to freeze across the Eastern Shore. The municipal water system in Millington (Kent County) developed leaks. A water main burst in Chesapeake City (Cecil County). Actual lowest temperatures included 8 degrees at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport, 10 degrees in Easton (Talbot County), 11 degrees in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County) and Salisbury (Wicomico County).

A fast moving Alberta Clipper low pressure system dropped 1.5 to 3.5 inches of snow across much of the Maryland Eastern Shore overnight on the 6th into the morning of the 7th. Slightly lower accumulations occurred in Cecil County. Snow began falling around 11 p.m. EST on the 6th and ended between 6 a.m. and 8 a.m. EST that morning. Temperatures were below freezing as the snow fell and it quickly accumulated on all surfaces. Untreated roadways and walkways were all slippery. Specific accumulations included 3.5 inches in Chestertown (Kent County), 2.0 inches in Galena (Kent County), Henderson (Caroline County) and St. Michaels (Talbot County), 1.5 inches in Denton (Caroline County) and Easton (Talbot County) and 1.0 inch in Elkton (Cecil County). The Alberta Clipper responsible for the snow raced from South Dakota around sunrise on the 6th to just east of the southern Delmarva Peninsula on the morning of the 7th. The low pressure system was moving too fast to drop heavier snow across the region.

An arctic air mass that originated near the North Pole invaded the Maryland Eastern Shore on the 5th and 6th. The combination of the unseasonably cold air and gusty northwest winds produced wind chill factors as low as zero to 10 degrees below zero during the mornings of the 5th and 6th. The lowest temperatures occurred during the morning of the 6th and were around 10 degrees. The unseasonably cold weather caused many pipes and water meters to freeze across the Eastern Shore. Actual lowest temperatures included 8 degrees at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport, 10 degrees in Easton (Talbot County), 11 degrees in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County) and Salisbury (Wicomico County).

A strong cold front moved through the Eastern Shore during the afternoon of the 22nd. The northwest flow between a deepening low pressure at the head of the front and a high pressure system building southeast from Canada intensified during the night of the 22nd. Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph and included 53 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport, 46 mph in Salisbury (Wicomico County), 44 mph in Easton (Talbot County) and 43 mph in Tolchester Beach (Kent County).

A fast-moving Alberta Clipper brought snow showers and gusty winds to the Allegheny Front of western Maryland by 10:00 AM on January 9 through 9:00 AM on January 10. Average snowfall reports with this system ranged from 1 to 3 inches with locally higher amounts, including 1.5 inches in La Valle, MD.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in La Valle, MD, reported 1.5 inches of snow by 8:00 PM on January 9th.

A strong cold front pushed across the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon of January 30. Upslope snow showers persisted over the Allegheny Front and Potomac Highlands through the morning of January 29. Light snow showers producing accumulations between 1 and 2 inches occurred across western and central Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 1 mile east of Frostburg reported 1.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked across southeastern Canada during the 12th, which brought an arctic front across the Mid Atlantic region late on the 12th. As this occurred, a strong area of high pressure located across central Canada built down into the Northeast, allowing very cold air to spill southward ultimately leading to what is known as cold air damming. This occurs east of the mountains down through the Mid Atlantic states. Meanwhile, a Pacific storm slammed onto the California coast during the 11th and as it moved eastward, a new area of low pressure developed across southeastern Colorado and the Texas Panhandle. This storm became impressive as it moved into the Ohio Valley during the 13th. However as additional energy moved into the eastern part of the country, a new area of low pressure developed near the North Carolina coast late on the 13th. This allowed mainly light snow to overspread the region from south to north during the early morning hours of the 13th. As the secondary area of low pressure became more organized to our south, moisture began to fill in across North Carolina and Virginia and this moved northward. The arctic front that slipped through the state on the 12th was positioned across the southern Delmarva and this began a northward jog during the night of the 13th and the morning of the 14th. The intensifying area of low pressure tracked very near southern Delaware and then slid close to the New Jersey Shore during the morning of the 14th. This system continued to intensify as it moved into New England during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. The track of the storm kept winds at the surface from the northeast, which pretty much locked in the cold air at the surface while some warmer air worked in above the surface for a period of time. The upper-level system then passed overhead during the evening of the 14th, with the entire system pretty much a memory by late in the evening of the 14th.|Snow began across Maryland���s Eastern Shore from south to north between 6 AM and 7 AM EST on the 13th. There was a lull in the precipitation during the evening hours of the 13th, however the precipitation became widespread overnight and during the early morning hours of the 14th. As some warmer air began to move in above the surface, the snow mixed with and changed to sleet and freezing rain between 12 PM and 4 PM EST. The mixed precipitation continued during the overnight hours of the 13th and the morning of the 14th in the northern part of the Eastern Shore. Plain rain fell farther to the south. In the north, somewhat milder air made its way in for a brief time during the morning of the 14th to allow some plain rain to mix in. After rising a couple of degrees above freezing around Midday on the 14th, the temperatures dropped back below freezing by late afternoon of the 14th with some snow showers before the precipitation came to an end around 8 PM on the 14th. The most significant sleet and ice accumulations occurred in Cecil County where up to a quarter of an inch of ice downed trees and power lines. |In Cecil County, Delmarva Power reported that about 12,000 customers in lost power starting the night of the 13th. Ice and snow contributed to a number of accidents as well. The state police barrack in North East handled about a dozen minor traffic accidents as of the afternoon of the 14th. An accident on Route 213 near Interstate 95 at about 10:30 AM on the 14th sent two people to Union Hospital after their vehicle lost control on the slippery roadway and struck a state plow truck, but neither was seriously injured. Cecil County schools were sent home early on the 13th and had the day off on the 14th because of the winter storm. North East town government canceled its mayor and commissioners meeting and rescheduled it for February 20th. County government offices also were closed on the 13th and 14th. In Kent County, on the afternoon of the 13th, a vehicle was traveling south on Route 213 in Chestertown when it slid on a patch of ice on the Chester River Bridge, crossed the center line and struck another vehicle. This closed the highway for an hour. Occupants of one of the vehicles were taken to Chester River Hospital Center for treatment. The bridge was not reopened until both lanes of Route 213 were heavily salted. At 9 AM on the 14th on Route 213 just north of the Route 298 intersection, a car struck a utility pole. Injuries were reported. Farther to the south on the Eastern Shore, precipitation started as snow during the morning of the 13th, then changed to a mixture of sleet and light freezing rain. Precipitation changed to plain rain by the evening of the 13th.|Some snow/sleet accumulations included 6.0 inches in the city of Conowingo (Cecil County) and 1.7 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne���s County).

A low pressure system moving out of the central plains weakened as it pushed east across the forecast area. After snow began to fall across the region, warm air at low levels moved in above sub-freezing temperatures at the surface. Light to moderate snow showers caused minor accumulations during the late morning hours of January 21 before snow mixed with sleet and freezing rain and then changed over to freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of January 22. SKYWARN Spotters and Cooperative Observers reported a mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain and drizzle, with total accumulations ranging from 1 to 4 inches across the region. |Multiple car accidents between 3:00 PM and 4:00 PM on January 21 closed portions of Interstate 95 and the Baltimore-Washington Parkway. Minor injuries were reported. Minor accidents were also reported on the elevated portions of the Jones Falls Expressway. Montgomery County authorities reported nearly 150 calls between 2:00 PM and 6:00 PM for traffic incidents. |In Frederick County, MD., dozens of traffic accidents were called into state police; ten of those resulted in minor injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Frostburg, MD, reported 3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A reinforcing cold front crossed the Allegheny Front and Potomac Highlands, bringing gusty northwest winds and scattered snow showers to the region from mid morning January 25 through the morning of January 26. Snow amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches along the Allegany Front of western Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Frostburg, MD, reported 1.5 inches of snow. Another spotter in Eckhart Mines, MD, reported 2.3 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the Mid Atlantic region during the morning hours of January 28. The eastern Great Lakes remained unfrozen, allowing lake effect snow showers to develop across the Allegheny Front and Potomac Highlands during the mid afternoon January 28 through the early morning hours of January 26. Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches Frostburg, MD, in Allegany County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Police in Frostburg, MD, reported 1.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest flow and an upper level disturbance moving southeast from the Great Lakes produced heavy snow showers across Garrett county Maryland and Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia. Snow accumulations averaged 6 to 8 inches with up to 15 inches in the highest elevations.

A strong clipper system developed over the Great Lakes March 6th and dove south across the Mid Atlantic on March 7th. Snow began along the western edge of the Allegheny Front shortly after midnight on March 7th and spread east overnight. |The highest snowfall reports came from Allegany County. Trained spotters in Frostburg, MD., reported 10 inches of snow. Snowfall amounts diminished further east, with an average of 1 to 3 inches reported across the Baltimore metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotters reported between 6 and 10 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area moved rapidly from the Great Lakes region southeast across the mid atlantic region then off the coast during Wednesday, March 7th. The system produced one to three inches of snow across portions of the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Linkwood reported 1.5 inches of snow, and Cambridge reported 1.0 inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong high pressure system to the north and a nor'easter low pressure system that passed to the east of Delaware produced a wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain across northern parts of the Eastern Shore and heavy rain across the southern part of the Eastern Shore. The heaviest rain fell during the day and early evening on the 16th. Storm totals averaged two to three inches across the region. Farther to the north sleet accumulations reached up to three inches in Cecil County with ice accretions generally less than one tenth of an inch.|Precipitation started as rain across the Eastern Shore during the evening of the 15th. But as colder air moved in aloft, precipitation changed to sleet during the morning of the 16th in Cecil County and in Kent County by the afternoon of the 16th. As the low pressure system tracked closer to the state during the afternoon of the 16th, the change to sleet stopped in Kent County. As the low moved farther offshore, and the winds backed more to the north, colder air filtered south into Queen Anne's County and changed precipitation over to sleet and freezing during the evening of the 16th. Precipitation fell as mainly rain in Talbot and Caroline Counties. All precipitation ended across the Eastern Shore by 1 a.m. EDT on the 17th.  | |In Cecil County, the Maryland State Route 213 Bridge was closed over the Chesapeake and Delaware Canal at Chesapeake City during the afternoon because of a crash. Precipitation storm totals included 3.00 inches in Federalsburg (Caroline County), 2.80 inches in Salisbury (Wicomico County) and 2.73 inches at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport. Sleet accumulations included 3.0 inches in Conowingo (Cecil County) and 2.0 inches in Elkton (Cecil County). |The wintry mix and heavy rain were caused by a nor'easter low pressure system that developed on a cold front that moved through the area on the 15th. Prior to that, unseasonably mild air helped push high temperatures as high as the 70s. A strong high pressure system moved across nearby Canada and supplied a fresh supply of cold air into the region. Meanwhile, the low pressure system formed over South Carolina and Georgia on the morning of the 16th and moved northeast. At 2 p.m. EDT on the 16th, it was near Myrtle Beach, South Carolina; at 8 p.m. EDT that evening, it was just east of Virginia Beach; at 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, it was about 100 miles east of Atlantic City, New Jersey and was about 100 miles south of Cape Cod, Massachusetts at 8 a.m. EDT on the 17th. As central pressures go, this was not particularly a powerful system; it was only 996 millibars the morning of the 17th. What contributed to the event was the strong high pressure system (about 1040 millibars the morning of the 16th). It supplied the fresh cold air needed to change the precipitation over to sleet and freezing rain and increased the pressure gradient (and consequently the wind) between itself and the developing nor'easter low pressure system.

Unseasonably colder air moved over western Maryland on April 4th and spread east through April 10th. Clear skies and light winds overnight allowed temperatures to fall into the upper 20s to lower 30s. Due to warmer temperatures at the end of March, many crops and gardens began to bloom early, before the established growing season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Saturated ground and light winds allowed dense fog to develop. Automated surface observations reported visibilities of a quarter mile or less during the late evening hours of March 24th through mid morning on March 25th.

An intense area of low pressure on the 1st moved into the Upper Midwest with a cold front stretched southward across the Ohio Valley all the way to the Gulf of Mexico. Meanwhile, another cold front moved through the Northeast and settled into the Mid Atlantic region. This boundary remained stationary for a time during the overnight hours of the 1st, as the strong low pressure system moved into the western Great Lakes. Gradually an occluded front moved through the area during the morning hours of the 2nd, as a weak area of low pressure developed over eastern Pennsylvania. Strong southerly winds developed between about 6:00 AM and 7:00 AM EST on the 2nd as the region got into the warm sector (south of a warm front and ahead of a cold front). Wind gusts were clocked to around 45 mph in some areas. The strong winds diminished by midday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly winds developed on the 2nd, generally between 6:00 AM and 7:00 AM EST, as a warm front moved north of the region and a cold front approached from the west. Winds gusted to around 45 mph in some areas, then tended to diminish by midday. A few wind gusts include, 41 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport and 47 mph in Salisbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably colder air moved over western Maryland on April 4th and spread east through April 10th. Clear skies and light winds overnight allowed temperatures to fall into the upper 20s to lower 30s. Due to warmer temperatures at the end of March, many crops and gardens began to bloom early, before the established growing season.

An intense nor'easter brought heavy rain and flooding to the Maryland Eastern Shore on the 15th and strong winds to the region on the 16th. Rain began falling during the evening on the 14th, but fell at its heaviest from about Midnight EDT through about 3 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The rain ended before sunrise during the early morning of the 16th. Event precipitation totals averaged between 3 and 6 inches. The strongest winds occurred as the nor'easter pulled northeast of the region on the 16th from the early morning into the afternoon. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph.|The worst reported flooding was in Cecil County on the 15th where four roadways were closed (including Delaware Avenue) in Elkton as the Big Elk Creek flooded. Flooding was also reported along Maryland State Route 7 at Broad and Peddler Creeks in Charlestown. The Trinity Woods subdivision experienced yard and roadway flooding. Elsewhere similar flooding conditions occurred as mainly poor drainage, yard and field flooding was reported. The gusty northwest winds on Monday the 16th caused scattered power outages for both Delmarva Power and Choptank Electric Cooperative.|Storm totals included 5.63 inches in American Corner (Caroline County), 3.80 inches in Federalsburg (Caroline County), 3.50 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County) and 3.37 inches at the Conowingo Dam (Cecil County). Peak wind gusts included 54 mph in Salisbury (Dorchester County), 53 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport, 48 mph in Easton (Talbot County), 47 mph in Annapolis (Anne Arundel County) and 46 mph in Chesapeake City (Cecil County) and Tolchester Beach (Kent County). |The nor'easter low pressure system initially formed in the southern Rockies on the 12th and moved to Arkansas on the morning of the 14th. At 8 p.m. EDT on the 14th, there were two low pressure systems, one in Kentucky and the other in western Alabama. The southern low pressure system became the predominate one overnight and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 15th, it (996 millibar low) was located in western North Carolina. As the 15th continued, it moved northeast and intensified rapidly. At 2 p.m. EDT the nor'easter (989 millibars) was near Raleigh, North Carolina. At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated Observations from the Greater Cumberland Regional Airport reported dense fog, reducing visibilities to one quarter mile or less.

The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.

An intense nor'easter brought heavy rain and flooding to the Maryland Eastern Shore on the 15th and strong winds to the region on the 16th. Rain began falling during the evening on the 14th, but fell at its heaviest from about Midnight EDT through about 3 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The rain ended before sunrise during the early morning of the 16th. Event precipitation totals averaged between 3 and 6 inches. The strongest winds occurred as the nor'easter pulled northeast of the region on the 16th from the early morning into the afternoon. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph.|The worst reported flooding was in Cecil County on the 15th where four roadways were closed (including Delaware Avenue) in Elkton as the Big Elk Creek flooded. Flooding was also reported along Maryland State Route 7 at Broad and Peddler Creeks in Charlestown. The Trinity Woods subdivision experienced yard and roadway flooding. Elsewhere similar flooding conditions occurred as mainly poor drainage, yard and field flooding was reported. The gusty northwest winds on Monday the 16th caused scattered power outages for both Delmarva Power and Choptank Electric Cooperative.|Storm totals included 5.63 inches in American Corner (Caroline County), 3.80 inches in Federalsburg (Caroline County), 3.50 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County) and 3.37 inches at the Conowingo Dam (Cecil County). Peak wind gusts included 54 mph in Salisbury (Dorchester County), 53 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport, 48 mph in Easton (Talbot County), 47 mph in Annapolis (Anne Arundel County) and 46 mph in Chesapeake City (Cecil County) and Tolchester Beach (Kent County). |The nor'easter low pressure system initially formed in the southern Rockies on the 12th and moved to Arkansas on the morning of the 14th. At 8 p.m. EDT on the 14th, there were two low pressure systems, one in Kentucky and the other in western Alabama. The southern low pressure system became the predominate one overnight and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 15th, it (996 millibar low) was located in western North Carolina. As the 15th continued, it moved northeast and intensified rapidly. At 2 p.m. EDT the nor'easter (989 millibars) was near Raleigh, North Carolina. At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.

Unseasonably colder air moved over western Maryland on April 4th and spread east through April 10th. Clear skies and light winds overnight allowed temperatures to fall into the upper 20s to lower 30s. Due to warmer temperatures at the end of March, many crops and gardens began to bloom early, before the established growing season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong clipper system developed over the Great Lakes March 6th and dove south across the Mid Atlantic on March 7th. Snow began along the western edge of the Allegheny Front shortly after midnight on March 7th and spread east overnight. |The highest snowfall reports came from Allegany County. Trained spotters in Frostburg, MD., reported 10 inches of snow. Snowfall amounts diminished further east, with an average of 1 to 3 inches reported across the Baltimore metro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management Officials reported between 1 and 2 inches of snow across Carroll County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure moving from the middle Mississippi Valley into the Upper Midwest brought a warm front across the Mid Atlantic on the evening of March 1st. A strong cold front crossed the region during the early morning hours of March 2nd. These two systems brought heavy rain and thunderstorms to the region. Roads were flooded in Frederick and Carroll Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management Officials reported flooded roads in Walkersville, Thurmont and Emmitsburg.|

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed due to flooding in Taneytown and Pleasant Valley. A total of seventeen roads were closed due to flooding county wide according to Emergency Management Officials.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed south across the Mid Atlantic during the afternoon of March 5th. While there was no precipitation associated with the front, strong gusty winds affected the region during the afternoon of March 5th and even into March 6th. These strong winds downed trees and power lines in Frederick County on March 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to a Newspaper report, around 125 Allegheny Power customers lost power when strong winds downed a tree onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of the strong northwest winds and an unseasonably cold air mass produced wind chill factors in the single numbers across the Eastern Shore on the morning of the 6th. Actual low temperatures were close to 20 degrees.

Strong west to northwest winds occurred across the Maryland Eastern Shore from the afternoon of the 5th into the morning of the 6th. The strongest winds occurred in two surges: when the front first moved through during the early afternoon on the 5th and then overnight when the cold air advection was at its peak. The air mass was very unseasonably cold for early March. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph. Around 5 a.m. EST on the 6th, the strong winds ripped the roof away from the bleachers at the baseball fields at the Martin Sutton Memorial Park in Ridgely (Caroline County). The grandstand behind the backstop of the baseball was knocked down and the wooden roof on top of the backstop crashed to the ground. Peak wind gusts included 46 mph at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport and 45 mph in Tolchester Beach (Kent County).

Upslope snow showers developed during the early morning hours of March 17th and continued through the late evening along the western slopes of the Allegheny Front in the wake of a strong cold front. The cold front and associated low pressure system brought several inches of snow to the region on March 16th before upslope showers developed several hours later. Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 5 inches across Allegany and Washington Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cooperative Observers reported between 3 and 5 inches of snow near Frostburg, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper low pressure system dropped light snow across the Eastern Shore during the day on the 7th. Accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches. The recent unseasonably cold weather made it easier for the snow to accumulate on roadways and bridges and overpasses during the early morning and also during heavier bursts of snow. Consequently untreated roadways were slippery. Snow began falling before sunrise on the 7th in Cecil County and spread south during the morning and began in Caroline and Talbot Counties by 9 a.m. EST. The snow ended in a similar direction (from north to south) during the mid and late afternoon of the 7th. Actual accumulations included 2.8 inches in Denton (Caroline County), 2.0 inches in Easton (Talbot County) and Ridgely (Caroline County), 1.8 inches in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County), 1.0 inches in Elkton (Cecil County) and 0.5 inches in Chestertown (Kent County).|The Alberta Clipper as it name implies moved rapidly southeast from southeastern Montana at 7 a.m. EST on March 6th, to near Saint Louis, Missouri at 7 a.m. EST on March 7th, to the Virginia/West Virginia border at 1 p.m. EST on March 7th, to about 150 miles east of Virginia Beach, Virginia at 7 p.m. EST on March 7th. The rapid movement of the low pressure system prevented heavier snow from falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid air mass developed as high pressure moved offshore and a cold front approached from the west. Scattered thunderstorms developed across central Maryland during the afternoon of June 27th. Some storms became severe, producing damaging winds across lower southern Maryland. One fatality occurred due to a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm, with nearly continuous lightning, moved out of Washington DC into adjacent western Prince Georges County, near Oxon Hill, MD. A 16 year old girl was struck by lightning waiting for a bus on Kennebec Street. She was later pronounced dead at a nearby hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of unseasonably cold air aloft and daytime heating helped produce some strong to severe thunderstorms across the Maryland Eastern Shore on the afternoon of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell in Elkton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of unseasonably cold air aloft and daytime heating helped produce some strong to severe thunderstorms across the Maryland Eastern Shore on the afternoon of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs and wires were knocked down by a severe thunderstorm in Cecilton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of unseasonably cold air aloft and daytime heating helped produce some strong to severe thunderstorms across the Maryland Eastern Shore on the afternoon of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SKYWARN Spotter reported nickel size hail fell in Queenstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of unseasonably cold air aloft and daytime heating helped produce some strong to severe thunderstorms across the Maryland Eastern Shore on the afternoon of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in Easton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in Rising Sun, Conowingo and Colora.

Three farm workers were struck and injured by lightning in Galena. They were entering a van at a nursery as the lightning struck. All three were taken to the hospital, but only one person was seriously injured.
An approaching cold front helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms during the evening of the 28th in the Maryland Eastern Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and wires in and around Chestertown. Power was lost at some homes and businesses for up to 10 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with an upper level low pressure system to trigger showers and thunderstorms along the Blue Ridge Mountains of Maryland during the late evening hours of July 28th. Some of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds in Washington and Frederick Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington County Emergency Management reported a tree down at the intersection of Marsh Pike and Leitersburg Pike northeast of Hagerstown, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front combined with an upper level low pressure system to trigger showers and thunderstorms along the Blue Ridge Mountains of Maryland during the late evening hours of July 28th. Some of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds in Washington and Frederick Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frederick County Emergency Management reported trees down in Ijamsville and Frederick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed across the Mid Atlantic on the evening of June 8th. Ahead of the front, a warm and unstable airmass allowed scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop over northern and central Maryland during the afternoon and spread southeast into lower southern Maryland during the evening. Some of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds that downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montgomery County Emergency Management reported power lines down from northern Bethesda to Rockville.
