Some of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds that downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Frederick County 911 Center reported trees down from Wolfsville to Thurmont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and humid airmass on the afternoon of June 11th allowed isolated showers and thunderstorms to develop over lower southern Maryland. Dry air in the mid levels of the atmosphere caused these storms to produce hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Trained Spotter estimated quarter size hail in Dentsville, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper Level disturbances combined with a warm and unstable airmass to produce widespread showers and thunderstorms across the Mid Atlantic. Some of these storms became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds across much of Maryland during the afternoon and evening of June 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management officials reported numerous trees down along Route 144 and Interstate 68 near Flintstone, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weak surface low pressure troughs over the Mid Atlantic triggered numerous showers and thunderstorms across western and central Maryland. These storms moved east into the Baltimore Metro area and south into lower southern Maryland. Some of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newspaper articles quoted a Law Enforcement Spokesperson reporting flooding. Green Street and Kelly Road were both closed for more than an hour when they flooded at the railroad trestles. A car was stuck in high water in the Virginia Ave. subway at Industrial Bldv. Numerous reports of flooded basements were also received by Emergency Officials.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably dry July was taking its toll on non-irrigated crops across the Maryland Eastern Shore from Kent County southward. Farmers were estimating their losses at 30 to 60 percent. The unseasonably dry weather also led to an explosion in the spider mite and grasshopper populations which further damaged crops. It was believed even if normal rainfall returned in August, it would be too late to help. Maryland Governor Martin O'Malley sent a letter to the Secretary of the USDA requesting that federal officials declare a drought disaster. This declaration would make low interest federal loans available to farmers whose crops have been compromised by the drought. On a county-wide average, July precipitation totals ranged from 2.1 inches in Kent County down to just 1.2 inches in Talbot County. The normal July rainfall for Kent, Queen Anne's, Talbot and Caroline County is either 4.0 or 4.2 inches.

A heat wave brought unseasonably hot weather to the Eastern Shore on July 8th through the 10th. The highest temperatures were on the 9th and 10th and were mostly in the mid to upper 90s. The combination of the heat and humidity produced afternoon heat indices of around 100F both afternoons. Cloudiness that preceded a cold front on the 11th prevented most places from reaching 90F again. Portable air condition sales jumped about 50 percent.||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees in Salisbury (Wicomico County), 97 degrees at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport and Easton (Talbot County) and 94 degrees in Fair Hills (Cecil County).

Portable air condition sales jumped about 50 percent.||Highest temperatures included 98 degrees in Salisbury (Wicomico County), 97 degrees at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport and Easton (Talbot County) and 94 degrees in Fair Hills (Cecil County).

A warm and humid air mass over the region allowed dense fog to develop along the Allegheny Front in western Maryland. Automated Surface Observations reported dense fog, reducing visibilities to a quarter mile or less during the early morning hours of August 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated Surface Observations reported dense fog, reducing visibilities to one quarter mile or less along and west of the Allegheny Front in Allegany County, MD.

An unseasonably dry July was taking its toll on non-irrigated crops across the Maryland Eastern Shore from Kent County southward. Farmers were estimating their losses at 30 to 60 percent. The unseasonably dry weather also led to an explosion in the spider mite and grasshopper populations which further damaged crops. It was believed even if normal rainfall returned in August, it would be too late to help. Maryland Governor Martin O'Malley sent a letter to the Secretary of the USDA requesting that federal officials declare a drought disaster. This declaration would make low interest federal loans available to farmers whose crops have been compromised by the drought. On a county-wide average, July precipitation totals ranged from 2.1 inches in Kent County down to just 1.2 inches in Talbot County. The normal July rainfall for Kent, Queen Anne's, Talbot and Caroline County is either 4.0 or 4.2 inches.

A hot and humid air mass developed over the Mid Atlantic ahead of a strong cold front on August 25th. Temperatures climbed into the mid 90s by noon. These temperatures combined with high humidity created heat index values around 105. Showers and thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon as the cold front approached the region. Some of these storms became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds that downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated Surface Observations reported high temperatures and relative humidities that produced heat index values of 105 and above.

A portion of the Mid Atlantic hydrologic service area experienced severe agricultural drought conditions from July 17th through the end of the month. This area included lower southern Maryland. Using a combination of rainfall measurements and radar precipitation estimates, some locations averaged rainfall totals as high as 6 inches below normal. This led to some jurisdictions restricting water use. Damage estimates were not available due to current harvest progress. Several locations were included in primary natural disaster areas due to reductions in farm production. Sources of information included the Maryland Farm Service Agency and the National Drought Monitor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While October saw the return of widespread above normal precipitation to Maryland for the first time since April, the crop damage was already done. The summer of 2007 was the second driest summer on record for the state since 1895. ||On October 9th, a drought watch was issued for the Eastern Shore and residents were urged to voluntarily conserve water. Near record low for October stream flows were recorded on the Big Elk Creek in Cecil County. ||Yields for corn and soybeans varied from farm to farm depending upon thunderstorms. Overall yields were down from 2006 with the soybean crop affected harder because of its later planting into the heart of the drought. The crop was also affected by spider mites and thrips. The crop damage was already done. On August 22nd, the United States Department of Agriculture declared the entire state a drought disaster area. This made farmers eligible for low interest loans. The corn harvest was about 40 percent below the level of 2006. About 20 million less bushels were harvested. The soybean harvest was about 34 percent below the level of 2006. Farms with sandy soil were hit the hardest by the drought. One of the few benefits of the dry weather was that it reduced powdery mildew problems on pumpkins and there were no muddy field problems. Pumpkin sizes were down because of the reduced rainfall. The apple crop quality in Cecil County was up from 2006. Orchards, blueberries, trees and shrubs were expected to show signs of stress in 2008. Farmers were contemplating on whether to even bother planting winter wheat. The entire Christmas tree planting was lost in Caroline County. Established trees survived, but the new plantings did not.

Orchards, blueberries, trees and shrubs were expected to show signs of stress in 2008. Farmers were contemplating on whether to even bother planting winter wheat. The entire Christmas tree planting was lost in Caroline County. Established trees survived, but the new plantings did not.

Unseasonably dry weather into August took its toll on non-irrigated crops across the Maryland Eastern Shore. While precipitation for the month returned closer to normal (county weighted averages were 3.1 inches to 4.1 inches), the damage was already done. On August 22nd, the United States Department of Agriculture declared the entire state a drought disaster area. This made farmers eligible for low interest loans. The Maryland Eastern Shore was one of the hardest hit locations in the state. Crop losses in Queen Anne's County reached 68 percent. Many farms lost between 30 and 60 percent of their crops. Even Christmas tree farms were affected. While the drought for the most part has been an agricultural concern, Preston (Caroline County) imposed odd/even watering restrictions.

An unseasonably hot and humid air mass affected the Eastern Shore on the 25th. While high temperatures were mainly in the lower to mid 90s, dew point temperatures averaged in the mid to upper 70s. This produced afternoon heat indices of 105F to 110F which were similar only to August 8th as the highest of the summer. A cold front moved through the area early on the 26th and limited this event to only one day.||Highest temperatures included 96 degrees at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport, 95 degrees in Easton (Talbot County) and Salisbury (Wicomico County) and 91 degrees in Fair Hills (Cecil County).

An area of low pressure moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the morning of August 25th. A cold front associated with this low pressure system was located in the Ohio Valley. A hot and especially humid airmass was found ahead of this front across the Middle Atlantic region on August 25th. As the front approached, showers and thunderstorms developed and moved across the area. The actual cold front however did not clear the region until the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flooding of smaller streams, fields and poor drainage areas in west central Caroline County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates were 3 to 5 inches.

This produced afternoon heat indices as high as 105F to 110F on the 8th.  ||Highest temperatures included 102 degrees at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport, 100 degrees in Stevensville (Queen Anne's County), 99 degrees in Easton (Talbot County) and 98 degrees in Salisbury (Wicomico County). A cold front moved through the area late in the night of the 8th. While there were some high temperatures in the lower 90s on the 9th, humidity levels were much lower.

An area of low pressure moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the morning of August 25th. A cold front associated with this low pressure system was located in the Ohio Valley. A hot and especially humid airmass was found ahead of this front across the Middle Atlantic region on August 25th. As the front approached, showers and thunderstorms developed and several of the storms became severe. The actual cold front however did not clear the region until the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm rumbled through Kent County during the evening of the 25th. Strong winds associated with this severe thunderstorm knocked down trees on Eastern Neck Island at 9:00 PM EDT.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved through a portion of Caroline County during the evening of the 25th. Strong winds produced by this severe thunderstorm knocked down trees in Ridgely at about 9:54 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the morning of August 25th. A cold front associated with this low pressure system was located in the Ohio Valley. A hot and especially humid airmass was found ahead of this front across the Middle Atlantic region on August 25th. As the front approached, showers and thunderstorms developed and several of the storms became severe. The actual cold front however did not clear the region until the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore through Queen Anne's County during the evening of the 25th. Damaging winds associated with this severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and power lines throughout the county. The most damage was located in Centreville and also between Queenstown and Queen Anne. County Officials reported that there was structural damage to several barns along with overturned farm equipment. As a result of the damaging winds, two horses were killed while another horse was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably dry weather in September continued to take its toll on non-irrigated crops across the Maryland Eastern Shore. For the state of Maryland, it was the 3rd driest September on record dating back to 1895. Along the Eastern Shore, except for Talbot County, on a county weighted average, September rainfall was less than 75 percent of normal. County weighted monthly rainfall totals ranged 0.4 inches in Cecil County to 1.0 inch in Talbot County. Normal is about 3.6 inches.||The crop damage was already done. On August 22nd, the United States Department of Agriculture declared the entire state a drought disaster area. This made farmers eligible for low interest loans. The corn harvest was expected to be about 40 percent below the level of 2006. About 20 million less bushels were expected to be harvested. The soybean harvest was expected to be about 34 percent below the level of 2006. Farms with sandy soil were hit the hardest by the drought. The dry weather also caused field fires as combines sparked blazes as they were harvesting the crops. One corn field fire consumed five acres. This was the worst agricultural drought since 2002. The drought was also starting to affect streams and groundwater supplies as they were both below normal. One of the few benefits of the dry weather was that it reduced powdery mildew problems on pumpkins and there were no muddy field problems.||Actual monthly precipitation totals included 0.35 inches at the Baltimore-Washington International Airport. Normal is 3.98 inches. In Salisbury (Wicomico County), the monthly precipitation total was 1.49 inches. Normal is 3.73 inches. At the New Castle County Airport (Delaware), the monthly precipitation total was 0.49 inches, 4th driest on record. Normal is 4.01 inches.

The combination of moisture and a mostly clear and calm night lead to widespread dense fog development during the early morning hours across portions of lower southern Maryland and north central Maryland. Local airports and automated observations reported fog reducing visibility to a quarter mile or less.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated surface observations across Calvert County reported dense fog reducing visibilities to a quarter mile or less.

The storm system brought an average of two to three inches of rainfall to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts averaged between two and three inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system drifted slowly from New England towards the Great Lakes for several days. This combined with a weak surface trough to trigger scattered showers and thunderstorms across the Mid Atlantic. One storm over Allegany County produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Allegany County Emergency Management reported a tree down in Westernport, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A secondary cold front crossed the region late June 21st. A warm and humid airmass ahead of the front combined with the approaching front allowed thunderstorms to develop across the Ohio Valley and move southeast towards  northern and central Maryland. Some of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported trees and power lines down between Middletown and New Market.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of low pressure over the Southeast United States and a nearly stationary frontal boundary across the Middle Atlantic Region helped to produce heavy rain across portions of the Lower Maryland Eastern Shore. The storm system brought an average of two to three inches of rainfall to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts averaged between two and three inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of unseasonably cold air aloft and daytime heating helped produce some strong to severe thunderstorms across the Maryland Eastern Shore on the afternoon of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several large trees in Kingstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure across the western Atlantic brought hot and humid conditions to the Mid Atlantic on July 10th. Showers and thunderstorms developed along and east of the Interstate 95 corridor, including the Washington DC and Baltimore metro areas, in the very hot and unstable air mass ahead of a cold front approaching the region from the Ohio Valley. Some of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail across the eastern Baltimore metro and south across lower southern Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Trained Spotter reported trees down in Linthicum Heights, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening of July 4th and moved east across northern Virginia and western Maryland. Some became severe, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Trained Spotter reported penny to quarter size hail near Catonsville, MD.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary remained draped across the Mid Atlantic for several days at the end of May. Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed in a warm and unstable airmass on May 27th. Some of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds across northern and central Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported a tree down near the intersection of Falls Road and Mt. Carmel Road north of Butler, MD.
